note: Mathlib/Algebra/Order/Floor/Defs.lean:258 `def fract (a : α) : α := a - floor a`, docstring: 'Int.fract a the fractional part of a, is a minus its floor.' This is exactly the fractional-part function.
verify_note: decl exists (Mathlib.Algebra.Order.Floor.Defs); source and docstring confirm Int.fract is literally a - ⌊a⌋, matching Wikidata Q2366982 'fractional part' (excess beyond the integer part) exactly.

note: Mathlib/Analysis/Complex/UnitDisc/Basic.lean:30-31 `def UnitDisc : Type := Subsemigroup.unitBall ℂ`, docstring: 'The complex unit disc, denoted as 𝔻'. Defines the open unit disc in ℂ as a type with induced structure.
verify_note: decl exists (Mathlib.Analysis.Complex.UnitDisc.Basic); Wikipedia article's stated standard case is exactly the open unit disc in ℂ, D={z∈ℂ:|z|<1}, which is what Complex.UnitDisc formalizes.

note: Mathlib/Combinatorics/SimpleGraph/Acyclic.lean:465 `lemma Connected.exists_isTree_le (h : G.Connected) : ∃ T ≤ G, IsTree T`, docstring 'Every connected graph has a spanning tree.' This is spanning-tree *existence* only — no edge weights or minimality, so it is not the weighted MST optimization problem, just the underlying unweighted-graph fact it relies on.
verify_note: decl exists (Mathlib.Combinatorics.SimpleGraph.Acyclic); confirmed no weights/minimality in statement, so 'related' (not 'exact') is the honest label for an unweighted spanning-tree-existence lemma vs the weighted MST concept.

note: Field-level match. Spectral theory (spectrum of elements/operators) has no single dedicated top-level directory in Mathlib — it's spread across Mathlib/Analysis/Normed/Algebra/Spectrum.lean, Mathlib/Analysis/CStarAlgebra/Spectrum.lean, Mathlib/Analysis/InnerProductSpace/Spectrum.lean (spectral theorem), Mathlib/Analysis/Matrix/Spectrum.lean, etc. Mathlib/Algebra/Algebra/Spectrum (160 decls; verified as key libraries.Mathlib.modules.Algebra.sub.Algebra.sub.Spectrum in catalog/data/hierarchy.json) is the base definition of `spectrum`/`resolventSet` that all of these specialize, so it is the closest single formal home, though partial/incomplete as a container for the whole field.
verify_note: path confirmed as key libraries.Mathlib.modules.Algebra.sub.Algebra.sub.Spectrum (n_decls=160) in hierarchy.json; Basic.lean docstring explicitly says the file 'will serve as the foundation for spectral theory in Banach algebras', supporting the hedged field-level claim.

note: Mathlib/Probability/Distributions/Gamma.lean:128 `def gammaMeasure (a r : ℝ) : Measure ℝ`. The chi-squared distribution with k degrees of freedom is Gamma(shape=k/2, rate=1/2); Mathlib has the general gammaMeasure but no dedicated chiSquared definition/name, so gammaMeasure strictly generalizes it (no explicit specialization lemma exists).
verify_note: decl exists (Mathlib.Probability.Distributions.Gamma); grep confirms no chiSquared/ChiSquared decl anywhere in Mathlib, and Gamma(k/2,1/2) is indeed the standard chi-squared parametrization, so 'generalization' (not 'exact') is honest.

note: Mathlib/Algebra/BigOperators/Intervals.lean:187, docstring 'Gauss' summation formula': `∑ i ∈ range n, i = n * (n - 1) / 2`. This is exactly the closed-form value of the (n-1)th triangular number, but it is stated as a generic sum identity — Mathlib has no named 'triangular number' def/predicate, only this formula.
verify_note: decl exists (Mathlib.Algebra.BigOperators.Intervals) at line 187; formula ∑_{i<n} i = n(n-1)/2 = T(n-1) confirmed correct, and 'related' is appropriately conservative since it's an unnamed generic sum identity, not a named triangular-number def.
